front: What is an argument in propositional logic?
back: An argument is a sequence of statements ending with a conclusion. All statements but the final one are premises. The argument is valid if the premises imply the conclusion.

front: State Modus Ponens as a rule of inference.
back: Modus Ponens: Given p → q and p, you can infer q. In logical form: (p → q) ∧ p ⇒ q.

front: State Modus Tollens as a rule of inference.
back: Modus Tollens: Given p → q and ¬q, you can infer ¬p. In logical form: (p → q) ∧ ¬q ⇒ ¬p.

front: What is the Hypothetical Syllogism rule?
back: Hypothetical Syllogism: Given p → q and q → r, you can infer p → r. In logical form: (p → q) ∧ (q → r) ⇒ (p → r).

front: What is the purpose of a formal proof using rules of inference?
back: A formal proof establishes the validity of an argument by showing that the conclusion logically follows from the premises through a sequence of steps, each justified by a rule of inference or a logical equivalence.
